ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Boston Lobster Floating Noodle. FOODON_00002572 (noodle dish) used as closest parent category.

serving.description: 1 whole Boston lobster with noodles (shared dish for 2–3 persons)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Boston Lobster Floating Noodle consists of one whole Boston lobster (approximately 600–700 g total dish weight including noodles and broth), intended as a shared dish for two to three diners.

nutrient_source.title: Lobster, cooked (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #15147) combined with Noodles, egg, cooked (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #20113) and composite dish estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Boston Lobster Floating Noodle exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite: approximately 40% cooked lobster (USDA SR Legacy #15147: 98 kcal, 20.5 g protein, 0.6 g fat, 1.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g), approximately 45% cooked egg noodles (USDA SR Legacy #20113: 138 kcal, 4.5 g protein, 2.1 g fat, 25.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g), and approximately 15% sauce/broth components (soy sauce, oyster sauce, stock). Final blended values adjusted for sauce sodium contribution. Estimation methodology follows FAO/INFOODS recipe calculation guidance.

gi_source.title: Noodles (egg noodles, cooked) — GI analogous estimate; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Boston Lobster Floating Noodle. GI estimated at 40 based on cooked egg noodles (GI approximately 35–46, mean ~40, per Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of GI and GL Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The lobster and sauce components contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 76.8) / 100 = 30.7, based on 600 g serving with 12.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and seasoned broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should exercise caution with this dish as the estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 2,520 mg per 600 g serving) approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce preparation or limiting portion size is strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from egg noodles; high protein from lobster may moderate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the egg noodle component contributes a moderate glycemic load (estimated GL ~30.7 per full serving); portion control of the noodle component and pairing with the high-protein lobster may help moderate postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate dietary cholesterol from lobster; low saturated fat is a positive factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note the high sodium content of this dish, which poses a risk for blood pressure management; however, the low saturated fat content (approximately 0.4 g per 100 g) and high-quality lean protein from lobster are relatively favourable, and moderate consumption as part of a sodium-controlled diet may be acceptable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boston Lobster Floating Noodle is a premium Zi Char-style seafood noodle dish popular in Malaysian-Chinese and Singaporean seafood restaurants. The dish features a whole Boston lobster (Homarus americanus), typically halved and wok-cooked or braised in a rich, savoury superior stock flavoured with ginger, garlic, spring onion, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil. The cooked lobster is then placed atop a bed of silky, lightly blanched egg noodles that appear to 'float' in the aromatic broth — giving the dish its distinctive name. The texture profile combines the firm, sweet, and slightly briny flesh of the lobster with the soft, springy egg noodles and the deeply umami-rich sauce. Clinically, the dish is high in lean protein (approximately 11.8 g per 100 g) and provides notable amounts of vitamin B12, selenium, and zinc from the lobster, supporting neurological and immune function. However, the dish is high in sodium (approximately 420 mg per 100 g) due to the soy and oyster sauce base, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 40 (low) based on the egg noodle component, though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate at approximately 30.7. This dish is typically served as a shared centrepiece for two to three diners and is priced at a premium reflecting the cost of whole Boston lobster. It is not suitable for individuals with crustacean shellfish, gluten, soy, egg, or sesame allergies.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Boston Lobster Floating Noodle exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked lobster and cooked egg noodles, adjusted for sauce components. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (cooked egg noodles) per the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked lobster (#15147) and cooked egg noodles (#20113), weighted approximately 40:45:15 (lobster:noodles:sauce). No direct analytical data for this specific dish was found in any approved primary source. 2. Sodium value (420 mg/100 g) is elevated due to the soy sauce and oyster sauce components; actual restaurant preparation may vary significantly. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimation. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimation. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as lobster and egg noodles contain no industrially produced trans fats; any trace amounts from natural sources are below detection threshold. 5. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on cooked egg noodles per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for this dish exists. 6. The dish is not halal-certified as Boston lobster (Homarus americanus) is considered haram by some Islamic jurisprudence schools (Shafi'i school), though it is permissible under Hanafi school rulings; halal status is therefore marked false pending restaurant-specific certification. 7. Price SGD 98.00 per dish preserved from input source (field survey data, Marina Seafood House - Boat Quay). 8. FoodOn FOODON_00002572 and FoodEx2 A0CKP are used as closest parent codes; no exact ontology match exists for this composite dish.
